description:
You are a security auditor who has just received a plain-text snapshot of file and directory permissions from a third-party server.  
Your goal is to analyse that snapshot and produce a concise frequency report that shows how many times each unique permission string appears.

Initial data location  
• The snapshot is already stored in  
  /home/user/audit_samples/perms_report.txt  

File format of the snapshot  
• ASCII text.  
• One permission string per line.  
• Each permission string is exactly 10 characters long (the typical ls-l style field, e.g. “-rw-r--r--” or “drwxr-xr-x”).  
• No empty lines and no trailing spaces.

Task details  
1. Read /home/user/audit_samples/perms_report.txt.  
2. Calculate how many times every distinct permission string occurs.  
3. Sort the final result:  
   a. Primary key: descending numeric frequency (highest count first).  
   b. Secondary key (used only when frequencies are equal): ascending (lexicographic) order of the permission string.  
4. Create the directory /home/user/audit_results if it does not already exist (the agent must create it when necessary).  
5. Write the sorted frequency list to the file  
   /home/user/audit_results/permission_frequency.log  

Exact output requirements for /home/user/audit_results/permission_frequency.log  
• Plain ASCII text.  
• Each line must have exactly this structure (items separated by ONE single space, no extra leading/trailing spaces or tabs):  

  PERMISSION_STRING COUNT  

  Example line (illustrative): -rw-r--r-- 7  
• Every line must end with a single Unix newline “\n”.  
• Do NOT include a header row or explanatory text—only the data lines.  
• After the file is written, display its contents to the console so the human auditor can see the summary immediately.

Verification notes (what the automated grader will check)  
• The file /home/user/audit_results/permission_frequency.log exists.  
• It is readable by the current user and was created without elevated privileges.  
• Its content exactly matches the required sorted order and formatting rules.  
• No extra or missing lines, spaces, or characters.  
• The agent used standard command-line tools (e.g. sort, uniq, awk, etc.) to produce the result, but there is no restriction on which specific pipeline is chosen.
truth:
Snapshot content (already present before the task starts):
Path: /home/user/audit_samples/perms_report.txt  
Contents (10 lines, each ending with “\n”):

-rwxr-xr-x
-rw-r--r--
drwxr-xr-x
-rwxr-xr-x
-rw-------
drwxr-x---
-rwxr-xr-x
-rw-r--r--
-rw-r--r--
drwxr-xr-x

Correct, fully-sorted frequency list that MUST appear in /home/user/audit_results/permission_frequency.log after the agent finishes:

-rw-r--r-- 3
-rwxr-xr-x 3
drwxr-xr-x 2
-rw------- 1
drwxr-x--- 1

Each of the five lines above terminates with a single “\n” and there is no blank line at the end of the file.
name: task_000000_0f635e4e
